speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 5. Today is June 10th, 1996, and our survivor is Shoshana Kahn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was talking about [NON-ENGLISH]. And when [NON-ENGLISH] was taken, his mother went absolutely mad. And all the Vilna girls were sleeping in one bunch. They just threw themselves over her so the, the Germans shouldn't hear her screams. Tried to choke her or hid her head in the chest, and-- and then they somehow got her husband over. And I remember him come, this handsome young man, and his face was-- I, I don't know how to describe it. Is, is the horror in his face. And then the woman-- the lady broke lose-- loose. And she ran all over the camp yelling, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I see her running. She had long legs and long hair and a kerchief which flew in the wind. And she was yelling, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then the Germans said, OK, we take you to your [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was the last we saw from-- of her. One more memory from Kaiserwald which was outstanding, it was in the beginning when they still came over from-- on Sundays. And we had loudspeakers, and she used to pipe in music. And since it was Germany, we got the best. It was Wagner and Beethoven and you name it. And here we are standing on, in Kaiserwald, and it plays Ninth-- the Ninth Symphony by Beethoven over there. And they're singing, [SINGING IN GERMAN]. And there was one German Jewish guy who was just standing there, like-- in transfixed, listening to the symphony. And an SS man walked by, and the guy didn't see him. And the SS man gave him a kick because he didn't take his hat off or something. I, I watched that, and that's an-- one of those things that I remember. How he came down from Beethoven to a concentration camp with one kick. He did like that, oh, my god. It, it was amazing, the combination of Beethoven and the kick that was so incredible. It was totally oxymoronic. Beethoven and that kick. I-- you know, this is the picture I have, all the time in my head, we are standing like that. OK, that was-- and then things became-- the Russians came closer. I think that was the story. And they tried to bring us into Germany. The story was the camp was going-- we were all going somewhere. They took first the German-- all the Germans, [NON-ENGLISH] and the German guys were lined up. I saw them as they were taken somewhere, X and Hannes and all that. They said all their goodbyes, you know. And, and some of them we were glad to get rid of. And then we were lined up, and we were-- and we were leaving the camp, and my cousin was saying goodbye to her husband. He worked-- he was doing something on cars, so he was an important man. He wasn't executed yet. And [NON-ENGLISH], my friend [NON-ENGLISH] brother Max was there, and we were taken to the harbor to a boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reach the harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a minute. We were in Riga. As far as I remember, we walked. We walked to that boat, to the big, big ship. And there was something already going on, and that's a fact. There was a fracas between the SS and the, and the, and the Marines, the soldiers. The soldiers said they want to be on the boat to go back to Germany. The Russians are coming. They said they had us to take. And we were very important. And then we were loaded on the ship, and I remember the soldiers, the soldiers saying to us, the German soldiers, [GERMAN]. Anyway, we got on that ship and we were loaded, and we were down there where the stones and the dirt is. That's where we're lying, one next to another. Each one got a bed, or whatever, and we're lying there. We got a few pails. It was Yom Kippur, I think. It wasn't fall. It was Yom Kippur, I think. And we got this [INAUDIBLE]. And then we started the journey. And of course, everybody got seasick if you have got below. And a lot of vomiting was going on, and the pails were overflowing. And I started to, to, to bring those pails up all the time and hand it to something. And get up, as I had to do something. And then the sailors came, and the sailors said I should please come visit them. They took me to a spic and span, beautiful bathroom and said, you clean this bathroom. But they didn't give me any soap or nothing. I just sat there on the tub.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing happened. And then they opened the door, and they threw chocolates in and candy and looked at me and closed the door. I was bored, you know. I wanted to go back what I know, to my, to my family. I said, please, may I go back to my family? They looked at me questioningly, handed me another chocolate, and I was coming down there. I mean, I thought maybe they would have wanted to hide me there. But they didn't say it. They said clean the bathroom. With what? They didn't have-- and the bathroom was so shiny, you wouldn't believe it. So, but I was bored to death sitting on a, on a toilet seat. So we went down there. And when we arrived, when the boat landed, we got on, on, on, on not boats, but square thing where we were standing on. [INAUDIBLE] what do you call those things? I mean, there was not boats. There were just floating things. Like the, the levy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were not boats, they were not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rafts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, rafts. They loaded on rafts, so tightly that I could stand only on one foot in order not to fall in. I do remember that Gerda was with us, and Gerda was pregnant from Mike the, Mike the cook. And she folded the blanket, and she said-- she didn't talk to anybody anymore. I saw her standing, not a word. She was sitting there. Folded her-- and my little sister there, was my mom was sitting there, and I was standing. And it was night. It took a while. We went on the Weichsel, that is that, that, that river. And I was afraid to topple over and fall into the water. So I kept on singing all night to keep myself awake. I sang anything which came into my mind, all operettas and songs and whatnot, because I could stand only on one foot. And then a male's voice said to me, don't you worry, my child. One day you will eat tomatoes. I promise you. I never know who that man was, but I tell you, I have a great respect for tomatoes. A great respect. Every time I eat a tomato, I hear him saying, my child, one day you will eat tomatoes. I'm eating tomatoes. My goodness, do I eat tomatoes. We came to Stutthof, and that was a horror. I mean, that was-- Kaiserwald was bad, but Stutthof, I-- we felt, it didn't smell right. First of all, nobody went to work. We were sleeping three in a, in a, in a bunk, three people in one bunk. So we slept like that. Two like that, and, and one like one, and then the feet between us, then we exchanged. I had the upper bunk with a mother, three. We were three. Three or four. I think maybe the mother and two daughters. Or three-- but whatever. It was a lot in this narrow bank. And we had to change always positions with the feet. And that-- early in the morning, there, there were women who were prisoners there, very cruel people. Big, with boots. They came with cold pails of cold water, threw it, the water over us in our bunks, so we had to get out on appell all wet and freeze.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stripes. It was already the stripey stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wooden clogs. We got those clogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat? What was a meal like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing, with a piece of [INAUDIBLE] in the morning. And with something which was looked-- was meant to be margarine. I don't know what it was. Had a strange, strange taste. And some kind of watery something soup at night. And it was a miserable time, as they are-- I was scared of that when I really thought, that is it. I mean, it-- and then those horrid women. There was one Latvian Riga woman there, and she had a mom, and this horrible fat, big [NON-ENGLISH] was kicking the old mother around. And we all were standing around and had to watch. And this girl got up and kicked the woman back. That's one of my memories. Dr. Resnik was a Jewish-- the Jewish doctor in our camp. She was there, too, Dr. Resnik from Vilna, and she was friends with this woman, Riga woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? How was this woman treated after doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know if she-- they punched her back, whatever. But the woman survived, died of cancer in, in somewhere. She was very close with Dr. Resnik They had a very close relationship. There were a lot of lesbian relationships in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of them at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but in hindsight, I knew because people were telling me that what would happen there next door in the bed. There was a lot of giggling going on. I don't know what. What have it-- what have you. I mean, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] was a fine doctor. She was-- and she lives in America. And well, that's a story afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that woman-- where were we? In Stutthof. And one day, there was appell again where we lined up and somebody said, oh, they have a hundred women for some kind of a job. They picked them out. And one girl said she won't go without her mom. And that they wouldn't-- they didn't tolerate. So I fast volunteered, instinct, selfish, selfish. Swim on your own. You have to be on your own, not to be tied down. I rejoined those 100 women and said goodbye to my mom and my little sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left Stutthof then and got to a so-called [INAUDIBLE] camp somewhere outside Danzig where there were a hunderd women. We had an Austrian SS person running the camp who had, of course, an Austrian Jewish mistress, who was kind of cuddly, lovely woman, who had a sidekick, of course. That will happen, usually, who, who had the better, better quarter in that, in that barrack. And we get-- went to work every morning. And we had quite a few of German [GERMAN] someone, someone were old, some were young. And there was a long table where we ate our bread at night. We didn't get much extra stuff there, that we had to live on our rations, more or less. There were two things which happened there. There, not far from there, was a factory for, for beets, where they made sugarcane. And there was something dripping out the pipes which was, which was sweet and brown. We collected that in tins and put it on our bread. That was-- still remember that smell. That's what we got there. And it was clean. And then one day I got a cockamamie idea. I will never eat-- I will not eat for four days, my rations, and then eat it, the whole lot on once-- at once. When I went to look for it, it was gone. Learned my lesson. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always had the instinct to-- in the morning, you get up, you brush your teeth, you comb your hair, and you wash yourself. It's concentration camp or not, I did that. And we didn't have much water. We had ice. So I used to kick the ice and take the ice and wash myself. And one morning-- I, I remember that, that, that we had appell and that Austrians, somebody or other, or [INAUDIBLE] said, you should all take as an example of number so-and-so, which was me, washes self-- herself every morning with ice to stay clean and, and, and healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because if you do not stay clean, you may get sick. And when you are sick, we will have to send you back to Stutthof, and you know what that means for you. You see that? That was very-- I thought that was kind of a good warning. How did you brush your teeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finger. [POINTING] With salt, whatever I got. Brush your teeth, and then with the ice, or with the ice, whatever. I always, I always combed my hair, and you know, I learned that looks were quite important. And whatever says is superficial and people-- Americans are obsessed with good looks. Looks were important. If you looked neat and you looked somehow presentable, it was easier. I can't help it. It was somewhat easier. And if you were clean, you felt better. And if I had, in the morning, got up and it was-- that cold ice woke me up, my-- it was a better situation, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what we-- as I was saying, we got there. It was along the harbor and there were ships. And the sailors had their tins. They threw the garbage out. And in the tins on the bottom were little [? liverwurst ?], the little this and that which we grabbed out and-- so we, we managed. But one day, that thing was over, too, and we're handed, we were handed a, a a bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one interesting thing happened there. One day, the young [GERMAN] said to me, I have a wonderful surprise for you. You're going on an outing. You're coming with me to the city of Danzig. You're going to do as if you carry something for me, which I can't do, but I want you to have an outing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Danzig, the two of us. We went on a strassenbahn, on a trolley, and the-- I was not meant to be because everybody, with my uniform, I had to stand there in the back with her things. And she took me on a tour of Danzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that part was not as interesting as the reaction of the people in the, in the, on the trolley. I just looked at them, and they were so shocked. They sit like that. They sat like that. And I remember this one man sitting, sitting like that. They couldn't look at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your physical condition at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was not bad. I mean, I had all this sugar and the, and that stuff coming. And then coming from Kaiserwald with the sandwiches, I was not a Musel lady. See, I wasn't-- I was skinny but not a Musel person yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your hair like at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, without hair. No hair, no hair. Maybe little stubbles. Yeah, short stubbles, but not-- well, it started-- they only shaved us once, yeah? It grew in kind of blonde, very blonde. One other one of my good fortune. And I had this little kerchief and this striped uniform. And the, the people were totally in shock. So that, that stupid [NON-ENGLISH] persons kind of smelled it and said, let's go home. She didn't make a good impression. So that was the one outing I had, this strange-- And and then were-- we got the bread, and we were supposed to leave. And we left. We marched first to a, a camp called [NON-ENGLISH] that people came from all over. There was a girl from my, from my city who was lying in the snow-- it was winter-- without underwear, nothing. Without shoes, without socks. The feet were so blue-- Lea Binder-- and I had two pairs of pants. My mom gave me one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said Lea, get up, get up. She says, I cannot. I said Lea, I give you one pair of pants. She was-- her pussy was out. I said Lea, take my pants. She says, [NON-ENGLISH] why are you doing this? Why do you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Lea, get up, get up. No, I'm not getting up. I cannot. I don't care. So she stayed, but I gave her the pants so she-- at least she, she was covered. She was a, a girl with lots of curly hair, Lea Binder. Didn't make it, poor thing. Well, there were people from all over. There were people from our Kaiserwald camp, I remember. There was this mother and daughter from-- who lived-- or stayed in the bunk not across from me. The daughter used to get visits from her husband in the beginning. She was pregnant at this point. And the mother and daughter were fighting with each other. They went like cats, I don't know, over the bread, over everything. It was amazing how they changed into animals. They were screeching, and they were scratching. And then they were falling asleep, and then they were waking up and scratching. They were such a good family. I always-- the mother was so sweet and all that. And I watched it turned into animals. And then we had to march in that snow. That was the so-called death march. And where we slept were, were sheds where other death marchers went through on the, on the straw, which was already full of diarrhea and, and, and what have you. We had to find a spot there to sleep. And we all got diarrhea because I finished my bread fast. I wanted to have the big feast. And then we started to eat frozen vegetables which we found. And we got diarrhea. It was unbelievable. It was uncontrollable. Just was running down our legs. And I do remember that one morning, we were supposed to start the march, and I just had those cramps. And I just squatted down where I was standing. And there was a German soldier standing in front of me. And he did like that. That's what he did. And I said, that is it. That does it. It's over. I'm not human anymore. That is over. People are-- people can't stand the sight of me. Well, I was dirty and, and, and scrawny and, and full of, of sticky stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were marching through the snow, and there was this lady from our town. She was a nurse before. And I said, listen, you have some opium pills. Would you give me one? And she couldn't afford to give me one. She says no, I cannot afford it. What if I need it? Logical.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were going through a little town. And the, and the women were-- people from the town were out there and throwing things at us, bread. Now, I first thought they're making fun of us, because women were hopping and jumping and catching and crying and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how could they feed so many people? They had to throw us, like to, like to birds. I misinterpreted that. I'm sorry I have that in my story, that they make fun of it. Some of them were hysterical. They were laughing, I mean. It was such a sight. The women hopping and screaming and, and crying and grabbing the, the food, the bread out from each other's mouths. I mean, it was-- they were wild.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was this horrible-- to walk with those wooden clogs and the snow kept on packing. And they had, all of a sudden, a high leg and a short leg, and a high leg, and a short leg, and it was-- and the diarrhea was-- and air was cold, and it was snow, was blowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was this Polish guy standing on the street and said to me, what are you doing? Why are you walking with them? Why don't you just run away? So I said to him, where to? He said, here's my address. Come. When you manage to run away, come. I walk with that lady, with my opium lady. [NON-ENGLISH] was her name. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH] don't look right. I'm trying-- I'm going to run. When we got around the bend, I ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this big-- there was a hysteria. The Germans were hysterical, too. And the snow and the bicycles, and the women were yelling. So I ran into a shed and hid behind some wood. Packed up, those peasants have that. And when it was night and it was quiet, I went to that address. How I found the address in the streets, I didn't ask anybody. I went there. And the situation was not what I expected. There was a woman. She was crying and begging not to receive me. Tears were coming down. She was sitting there almost on her knees. All the women were crying. And he was standing there, this man, totally embarrassed, helpless. Then he said, you know, he said, you come with me. You may spend the night. But when it's-- when the light goes on, you are not-- I don't want to find you there. Well, I was-- he put me there in the pig shed, and there was a frozen something or other. I sat on it. He could have given me a piece of bread, or rather didn't. Maybe I didn't-- why would-- if I got found, they ask me where I found the bread. You know, that was all-- that there's-- there-- the punishment for hiding a Jew was draconian, draconian. Not only the person who hid me gets executed, everybody in the neighborhood did. That was their system. That's how they got it to the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat there and howled like a wolf. That was when I really cried. It was so-- I really thought why I almost, almost, where am I going to go? It's cold, and I'm hungry and the diarrhea and what. The light was on. I promised to go. I went. Where do I go back? It was Sunday morning, every-- the, the village was asleep. I don't know if people saw me from the windows. I went back to the shed where I, where I was at the first. I sat behind it, behind those wooden things as they pile them up. And then I heard, heard steps coming. At this point, I thought, what will be will be. If they shoot me, they shoot me. I mean, my god. How much, how much can I go through? But it was an old Polish man, and-- an old guy. And I never seen an expression like that in anybody's eyes, the empathy, the pain of recognizing a, a girl in that condition. He, he looked at me like he would have-- like, like an angel. He didn't say a word. Polish I didn't know. He took me by the hand and took me into the-- a peasant's house. Were you afraid to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No! What am I afraid-- now I'm afraid to go? I wasn't afraid. I-- look, what could happen to me? Only thing you get killed. What is there afraid to go? I mean, you finessed. 50 chance. It was always a 50-- just did finessing. I was not afraid. I know what, what can be when it's bad and what can be when it's not bad. Being afraid was your death knell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me to the peasant's house, and there was just the most amazing people, the most amazing people. There was the husband and wife and a daughter. They already were harboring a, a Polish officer in his uniform, which was really dangerous, a Russian pilot, and some Russian girl. I don't know what she, what she was doing there. And then now I came. They cleaned me. They fed me. They, they were-- What about your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? They took the uniform. They burned it. They washed me. They gave me food by the spoon, slowly. The Russian-- the anti-Semite Polish things, he was so mean. But he said, not too much food. Only one food, yeah. And then it was a-- what do you think you're doing here? You're supposed to work. You know, OK, so I did that with knitting. But they-- but they were wonderful. Absolutely wonderful, wonderful, wonderful people. Ah, the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the name and not the village, and that's it. I'm not supposed to know the name, but it was an agreement. Because if I get caught, yeah? And the village I knew for a while, and now I don't know it either anymore. And this is another one of my conscience, because I could have done so much for them. The daughter was my age. And then one morning, there was a razia. You know what that is. And the peasant came running in white. He said, disperse, disperse, fast, fast, fast. The Pole-- the Russian pilot-- oh, they-- they had their places already stacked out. They know where to go. And me he put in bed with his daughter, like a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans came, and they had [INAUDIBLE] they were looking for and laughing and kidding, and I had the [INAUDIBLE]. And he came out and tried to bribe the German with hams and sausages. And the German was like laughing at me, snickering at me. And I said-- well, who are you? What are you? Where is your passport? I said I-- up on the truck. We go to prison to Karthaus, was the next biggest city. Karthaus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the trucks they had some already-- some unlucky souls. And there was one Hungarian girl from, from the Kaiserwald. I remember her from there. And there was a whole [INAUDIBLE]. And she looked at me. I looked at her. Did as if we don't know each other. We got into prison, in a cell, the whole lot. And they-- one could see from the window that they put out people one by one and, and, and whatever. They executed them, what have you. And then the door-- we were less and less in the cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in the cell to begin with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I only know we were less and less. The Hungarian girl I remember. A, a boy, a, a blonde boy, me, and more, other people. You know, it's all-- you, you, you-- it is so strange. You don't know how. You think I sat there, I counted? [NON-ENGLISH] would have counted. He always knows how many. I know how it was. We were, we were-- this whole truck. And then somebody came with a list and said, anybody who comes from Germany, please step forward. Now, again, I was always told to tell the truth and not to lie. I had to-- really, to make myself step forward. And the boy did, the blonde boy, and I did. And we followed that guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the meantime, I have already my, my part laid out. You see, I studied [NON-ENGLISH], the smart girl. So always had it, all the role. I know my role. What would [? Catherine ?] have done? What would [? Catherine ?] have said? What did [? Catherine ?] do? So I didn't have to think much. So I got, got into the office with the Gestapo people, and I was interrogated. They bought a big map. They asked my name. So I know the only real German name from our town was Reimer, and Veronica sounded German enough. Veronica Reimer. Why did you pick that name, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- well, this I don't know. It sounded German. And then I was extremely clever about dialect. And I was a young kid. I was really proud of myself. I listened in what the-- what dialect those guys were talking in the office and those people interrogated. None of them talked the [NON-ENGLISH] I mean, Koenigsberg, [NON-ENGLISH], that whole Deutsch stuff we talked on the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: I took my-- I, I said I come from Koenigsberg. Never was there in my life. And I hoped nobody [INAUDIBLE] and nobody was from [? Koenigsberg. ?] And, and then, how did you get here? And I thought we were those-- at that time, already, the Germans were, were fleeing the Russians from here. And I was on a truck, and we were fleeing the German-- the Russians, and I got sick, and I stayed with those people who, who were taking care of me while I was so ill. And now I was picked up. And then this guy came again, what dates and so on. I said, listen, if I would have known you'll ask me a stupid question, I would have kept a diary. Ipso facto, [? Catherine ?] would have said that, too. And then they brought up the map and said, how did I come? I said, I don't know very much of geographies. Surely, I said, I, I, I lost my parents. I don't know where they are. I'm worried about them. OK. Then he said to me, never-- what did they arrest me for? As a Russian spy. Never as a Jewess. So if I said I'm not Russian, I didn't lie. Not at all. And I didn't know Russian then so much either, only a few words. I know Russian better now. So I did not lie. I was very confident. The only little-- that guy said, you know, I don't like your eyes. Something about your eyes I don't like. I said, do you really? I said, everybody admires my brown eyes and my blonde hair. Why don't you like it? Besides, I don't like your eyes. Your eyes are very dark. Ah, he was-- he said, at that point, I said, I send you to room 79-- or 75, something in the 70s-- where you're going to meet Commandant so-and-so from the Gestapo, and he's going to take care of you.
